summary: DeVault argues that true software portability comes not from supporting a few popular platforms, but from adhering to standards like POSIX. He contends that POSIX compliance means software runs on virtually every operating system and CPU architecture, both existing and future. He extends the argument beyond operating systems to browsers and networking protocols, claiming that failing to use standard interfaces is a form of vendor lock-in. He questions whether languages like Rust can claim systems programming suitability when they only support a handful of platforms, contrasting this with C's universal reach.
key_points: Portability is defined by adherence to standards, not by the number of platforms explicitly supported
POSIX is the most important portability standard, supported by a vast range of operating systems and architectures
Portable software enables innovation by giving new operating systems and browsers immediate access to existing applications
Failing to use standard interfaces is a form of vendor lock-in and is arguably unethical
Languages like Rust cannot credibly claim systems programming suitability while only running on a small number of platforms
money_quotes: Supporting multiple systems does not necessarily make your software portable. What makes your software portable is standards.
By supporting POSIX, your software runs on basically every system. That's what it means to be portable - standards.
The current market leaders are not the end-all-be-all of operating system design - far from it. What they have in their advantage is working well enough and being incumbent.
Windows, Linux, and macOS are still popular for the same reason that legislator you don't like keeps getting elected!
All you have to do to make your OS viable is implement POSIX and you will immediately open up hundreds, if not thousands, of potential applications.
I'd argue that failing to do this is unethical - it's just another form of vendor lock-in.

I question niche programming languages like Rust when they claim to be suited to systems programming or even kernel development. That's simply not true when they only run on a small handful of operating systems and CPU architectures.
key_insight: True portability comes from building on standards like POSIX rather than targeting specific platforms, and this matters because it enables innovation and prevents vendor lock-in.
